defs: A handle; a stale, or stele.
quotes: And in his hand a huge poleax did bear. Whose steale was iron-studded but not long.

records.ety: [OE. stelen, AS. stelan; akin to OFries. stela, D. stelen, OHG. stelan, G. stehlen, Icel. stela, SW. stjäla, Dan. stiaele, Goth. stilan.]
records.morph: [imp. Stole (stōl); p. p. Stolen (stō"l'n); p. pr. & vb. n. Stealing.]

defs: To take, and carry away, feloniously; to take without right or leave, and with intent to keep wrongfully; as, to steal the personal goods of another.
quotes: Maugre thy heed, thou must for indigence Or steal, or beg, or borrow, thy dispense.
The man who stole a goose and gave away the giblets in alms.

defs: To withdraw or convey clandestinely (reflexive); hence, to creep furtively, or to insinuate.
quotes: They could insinuate and steal themselves under the same by their humble carriage and submission.
He will steal himself into a man's favor.

defs: To gain by insinuating arts or covert means.
quotes: So Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel.

defs: To get into one's power gradually and by imperceptible degrees; to take possession of by a gradual and imperceptible appropriation; -- with away.
quotes: Variety of objects has a tendency to steal away the mind from its steady pursuit of any subject.

defs: To accomplish in a concealed or unobserved manner; to try to carry out secretly; as, to steal a look.
quotes: Always, when thou changest thine opinion or course, profess it plainly, . . . and do not think to steal it.
She yesterday wanted to steal a march of poor Liddy.
Fifty thousand men can not easily steal a march over the sea.

defs: To practice, or be guilty of, theft; to commit larceny or theft.
To withdraw, or pass privily; to slip in, along, or away, unperceived; to go or come furtively.
quotes: Fixed of mind to avoid further entreaty, and to fly all company, one night she stole away.
From whom you now must steal, and take no leave.
A soft and solemn breathing sound Rose like a steam of rich, distilled perfumes, And stole upon the air.
